coredata.dc:title: Core-shell structure N-doped graphene quantum dots Fe3O4/Co3O4 nanoparticles for colorimetric detection of H2O2
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yujin
Qiu, Mingzhu
Guo, Peiqing
Lei, Xuefang
Li, Shaohui
Meng, Ran
Chen, Nali
Zhang, Dongxia
Zhou, Xibin
coredata.dc:description: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is an environmentally friendly intermediate in the environmental field and also plays an important role in human metabolism, but the production and accumulation of excessive H2O2 will cause harm to the human body, resulting in serious damage to cells. Therefore, N-doped graphene quantum dots Fe3O4/Co3O4 nanoparticles with core-shell structure were prepared by in-situ synthesis and precipitation. The experimental results show that the prepared Co3O4 nanoparticles have more efficient peroxidase activity than Co3O4 nanoparticles alone. It can catalyze the oxidation of 3, 3, 5, 5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to produce a blue product (TMBox) in the presence of H2O2. In addition, Co3O4 with negative charge in the shell can produce a strong electrostatic driving coordination effect with positively charged TMB, which enhances the affinity between the nanoparticles and the substrate, and selectively catalyzes H2O2 oxidation of colorless TMB to turn it blue. Based on these experimental results, a simple, low-cost, and efficient colorimetric method for H2O2 detection was established, which showed a sensitive response to H2O2 in the range of 0.02–2.5 μM with a detection limit of 2.3 nM. In addition, due to the magnetic properties of the prepared nanoparticles, they could be easily recovered by applying a magnetic field. This research provides a viable approach for magnetic nanomaterials with encouraging prospects for environmental monitoring, biosensing, and more.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal oxide
Graphene quantum dots
Core-shell structure
Colorimetric detection
H2O2 detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Peroxidase mimetic activity of core-shell nanoparticles
Catalytic mechanism and steady-state kinetic studies

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

Key Laboratory of Resource Environment and Sustainable Development of Oasis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (201165016, and 21265018); the Science and Technology Support Projects of Gansu Province (Nos. 1011GKCA025, and 1208RJZM289); and the Postgraduate Research Funding Projects of Northwest Normal University (2021KYZZ02140), and Key Laboratory of Resource Environment and Sustainable Development of Oasis, Gansu Province (GORS202301).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM of (A) Fe3O4, (B) N-GQDs/Fe3O4, (C) Co3O4 (D) N-GQDs/Fe3O4/Co3O4 (E) Element mapping of Fe, Co, C, N, and O.
(A) XPS spectra of N-GQDs/Fe3O4@Co3O4, (B) C1s, (C) Fe2p, (D) Co2p, (E) N1s, (F) O1s.

UV–visible absorption spectra of different reaction systems (A) TMB+H2O2 (B) TMB+N-GQDs/Fe3O4/Co3O4 (C) H2O2+N-GQDs/Fe3O4/Co3O4 (D) TMB+H2O2+N-GQDs/Fe3O4/Co3O4 (E) TMB+H2O2+Co3O4 (F) TMB +H2O2+N-GQDs/Fe3O4.

EPR spectra of ·OH (A), ·O2 - (B), 1O2 (C) in the N-GQDs/Fe3O4/Co3O4 reaction system (D) Schematic illustration of peroxidase mimetic catalytic reaction process of the N-GQDs/Fe3O4/Co3O4 nanoparticles.

Steady-state kinetic analysis of N-GQDs/Fe3O4/Co3O4 nanoparticles: (A) 0.1 mM H2O2 plus different concentrations of TMB (C) 10 mM TMB plus different concentrations of H2O2 (B) and (D) are double inverse plots of (A) and (C) respectively.

(A) UV–visible spectra of the system in the presence of different concentrations of H2O2 (the inset shows the color change with the addition of different concentrations of H2O2 to the system) (B) Calibration curve of absorbance versus concentration of H2O2 (C) Selectivity of N-GQDs/Fe3O4/Co3O4 to H2O2 for various amino acids and ions, all at a concentration of 1 mM, and a concentration of H2O2 of 0.1 mM (D) Effect of storage time on N-GQDs/Fe3O4/Co3O4 (E) Absorbance change at 652 nm for the number of reaction cycles.
Schematic diagram of N-GQDs/Fe3O4/Co3O4 nano enzymes synthesis.

Comparison of parameters of K
m , V
m
and Kcat.
Comparison of detection limits of different methods for the determination of H2O2.

head.ce:title: Core-shell structure N-doped graphene quantum dots Fe3O4/Co3O4 nanoparticles for colorimetric detection of H2O2

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Geography and Environment Science, State Key Laboratory of Resource Environment and Sustainable Development of Oasis, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Geography and Environment Science, State Key Laboratory of Resource Environment and Sustainable Development of Oasis, Northwest Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Geography and Environment Science, State Key Laboratory of Resource Environment and Sustainable Development of Oasis, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Petrochemical Technology, Lanzhou University of Technology, Lanzhou 730070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Petrochemical Technology, Lanzhou University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Petrochemical Technology, Lanzhou University of Technology, Lanzhou 730070, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is an environmentally friendly intermediate in the environmental field and also plays an important role in human metabolism, but the production and accumulation of excessive H2O2 will cause harm to the human body, resulting in serious damage to cells. Therefore, N-doped graphene quantum dots Fe3O4/Co3O4 nanoparticles with core-shell structure were prepared by in-situ synthesis and precipitation. The experimental results show that the prepared Co3O4 nanoparticles have more efficient peroxidase activity than Co3O4 nanoparticles alone. It can catalyze the oxidation of 3, 3, 5, 5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to produce a blue product (TMBox) in the presence of H2O2. In addition, Co3O4 with negative charge in the shell can produce a strong electrostatic driving coordination effect with positively charged TMB, which enhances the affinity between the nanoparticles and the substrate, and selectively catalyzes H2O2 oxidation of colorless TMB to turn it blue. Based on these experimental results, a simple, low-cost, and efficient colorimetric method for H2O2 detection was established, which showed a sensitive response to H2O2 in the range of 0.02–2.5 μM with a detection limit of 2.3 nM. In addition, due to the magnetic properties of the prepared nanoparticles, they could be easily recovered by applying a magnetic field. This research provides a viable approach for magnetic nanomaterials with encouraging prospects for environmental monitoring, biosensing, and more.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Strong oxidizing agents like hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) are often employed in manufacturing, food processing, and medicines [1,2]. In addition, as an important intermediate in biological processes, it has also been associated with cell damage and many diseases. Due to its functions of disinfection and sterilization, bleaching, and preservation of freshness, it is widely used in the processing of dairy products and dried seafood and fruits to extend shelf life. However, excessive intake of hydrogen peroxide can cause irreversible damage to the human body, and therefore the FDA (U.S. Food and Drug Administration) stipulates that no hydrogen peroxide higher than 0.05 wt% should be used in food processing [3]. Therefore, the quantitative detection of hydrogen peroxide is of great importance. So far, many convenient and rapid methods have been developed for the determination of hydrogen peroxides, such as the fluorescence method [4], an electrochemical method [5], and a colorimetric method [6]. Among them, the colorimetric approach is the most straightforward, convenient, and efficient and can be seen with the unaided eye. In the colorimetric detection of H2O2, Horseradish peroxidase (HRP) is widely used for its simplicity, efficiency, and sensitivity. However, the inherent drawbacks of HRP, such as its activity being highly susceptible to environmental factors and difficult to fabricate and not easily storable, have limited its application in practice. For this reason, nanomaterial-based enzyme mimics (nano enzymes) with intrinsic enzyme activity were developed as alternatives to natural enzymes [7–9].

Co3O4 is a versatile metal oxide with good properties in catalysis, conductivity, and energy storage which is widely used in catalysts [10], supercapacitors [11], and sensors [12]. Due to having ion pairs with different valence states, it can promote electron transfer between some enzyme substrates, thus exhibiting enzymatic properties (such as peroxidase-like, superoxide dismutase-like, and catalase activities) [13]. In 2012, Mu et al. first synthesized cubic Co3O4 nanoparticles with peroxidase and catalase activity and used for the determination of glucose and hydrogen peroxide [14]. Unfortunately, Co3O4 nanoparticles are tend to aggregate together for the magnetic attractive forces and long-range van der Waals forces, thus resulting in weakening the enzyme-like characteristics [15]. In recent years, many efforts have been made to preventing them from aggregating. For example, Chen et al. developed novel palygorskite@Co3O4 nanocomposites (Pal@Co3O4) by a facile hydrothermal method for the detection of H2O2 and ascorbic acid (AA) [16]. Xie et al. prepared the Co3O4-reduced graphene oxide (Co3O4/rGO) by hydrothermal reaction for selective detection of glucose [17]. Ni et al. synthesized Co3O4-MCNFs nanocomposites consisting of Co3O4 nanoparticles and mesoporous carbon nanofibers and applied to the determination of H2O2 in spiked milk samples [18].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a zero-dimensional (0D) nanomaterial, have abundant active sites, excellent stability, and good biocompatibility, and can be used as an ideal carrier to provide large specific surface area to prevent metal agglomeration [19]. The abundance of oxygen-containing groups on the surface of GQDs increases the number of anchoring sites for the adsorption of metal ions. In addition, GQDs also have high surface energy and specific surface area, making it easier to interact with their surroundings [20]. Graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), as zero-dimensional (0D) nanomaterials, exhibit good enzyme activity due to their high specific surface area, and are also used in the synthesis of various nanomaterials [21–23]. For example, Zhang et al. prepared graphene quantum dots-copper oxide nanocomposites (GQDs/CuO) by microwave thermo-liquid method, and used for the detection of glucose in serum with efficient peroxidase activity [24]. Ju et al. prepared AgNPs-N-GQDs nanocomposites by photocatalytic method with N-GQDs as reductant which had an efficient catalytic activity for the detection of glutathione in serum [25].

Furthermore, compared to single nanomaterials, core-shell nanomaterials have more physical and chemical properties and will also maintain the integrity and stability of the nanomaterials, effectively preventing the aggregation of nanoparticles [26].

The citric acid (C6H8O7·H2O), urea (CH4N2O), ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6 H2O) were purchased from Aladdin Chemicals Ltd. (Shanghai, China), anhydrous sodium acetate (CH3COONa), trisodium citrate (C6H5Na3O7·2 H2O), glycol (C2H6O2), Cobalt nitrate (Co(No3)2·6 H2O) from Aladdin Chemical Co., LTD. (Shanghai, China), anhydrous sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), sodium acetate trihydrate (CH3COONa·3 H2O), acetic acid (CH3COOH), formic acid (CH2O2), glycine (Glycine), L-arginine (L-Arginine), L-histidine, L-Tryptophan, KMnO4, K2CrO7, TMB (3, 3′, 5, 5′ -tetramethylbenzidine), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Other solutions and metal ion solutions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Ca2+) were analytically pure and did not require further purification. Deionized water was used throughout the work.

Ultraviolet-visible absorption spectra were measured with Mapada (Shanghai, China) spectrophotometer. The powder diffraction data of the crystal products were obtained using the DMM-10 powder X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (40 kV, 30 mA, XRD). The products were imaged by Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) using JEOL F200 transmission electron microscopy (TEM). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is performed on the AXIS UltraDLD analyzer. Binding energy (BE) is regulated by unlabeled carbon (C1s, 284.6 eV).

According to a previous report, N-GQDs were made to be synthesized by a hydrothermal method [27]. 2.1 g of citric acid and 1.8 g of urea, were dissolved in 50 mL of water with stirring for 3 h. The mixture was transferred to a stainless steel autoclave with a polytetrafluoroethylene lining, and the mixture was heated at 160 °C for 8 h. The dialysis was carried out for 48 h, with a change of water every 12 h. The mixture was then transferred into a stainless steel autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene.

N-GQDs/Fe3O4 nanoparticles were prepared by in-situ synthesis. 0.675 g ferric chloride hexahydrate, 1.8 g sodium acetate anhydrous, and 0.01 g sodium citrate were mixed in 75 glycols, 3 mL N-GQDs was added, ultrasonic was carried out for 30 min, and the mixture was transferred to a stainless steel autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene at 200 ℃ for 16 h, and washed with ethanol and deionized water for several times.

N-GQDs/Fe3O4/Co3O4 nanoparticles were prepared by precipitation method. 0.038 g N-GQDs/Fe3O4 was dissolved in 5 mL glycol, and 0.076 g cobalt nitrate was dissolved in 0.92 mL glycol. Ultrasonic until evenly mixed, oil bath 160 °C, add 4.4 mL 0.2 M sodium carbonate solution drop by drop, stir for 1 h, centrifuge, wash with deionized water three times, dry, calcined at 450 °C for 4 h.

TMB and H2O2 were used as substrates to evaluate the peroxidase activity of core-shell nanoparticles. In the presence of H2O2, the nanoparticles catalyzed H2O2 to produce ·OH and oxidize TMB, resulting in the blue oxidation state TMB (ox-TMB). In a typical reaction, 1 mg/mL N-GQDs/Fe3O4/Co3O4 nanoparticle suspension, 0.1 M H2O2, 10 mM TMB, and HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH=6) were mixed in a centrifuge tube and incubated at room temperature for 5 min. The absorbance of the solution was monitored by a UV–visible spectrophotometer.

The peroxide-like catalytic properties of core-shell nanoparticles were measured by steady-state kinetics using H2O2 or TMB as substrates. Kinetic experiments were carried out at room temperature by adding 50 μL of H2O2 (1–5 mM) and TMB (0.125–0.25 mM) at different concentrations to 20 μL1mg/mL N-GQDs/Fe3O4/Co3O4 nanoparticle suspension and HAc-NaAc buffer (pH=6), respectively. The reaction is performed in a solution with a total volume of 2 mL. The absorbance of the solution at 652 nm was recorded every 30 s. The Michaelis-Menten constant (K
m ) can be obtained from the Lineweaver-Burk diagram.
v
0
=
V
max
*
S
K
m
+
S
1
v

=
K

+
S
V
max
*
S
Where v
0 represents the initial rate, V
max represents the maximum reaction rate, K
m represents the Michaelis-Menten constant, and [S] represents the substrate concentration.

Based on the linear relationship between H2O2 concentration and absorbance of ox-TMB at 652 nm, a colorimetric method for H2O2 detection using N-GQDs/Fe3O4/Co3O4 nanoparticles as peroxidase mimics was developed. Specifically, 20 μL N-GQDs/Fe3O4/Co3O4 suspension (1 mg/mL) and 50 μL TMB (10 mM) were added successively to the HAc-NaAc buffer solution (1880 μL, pH=6), and finally, H2O2 solution with different concentrations was added and reacted at room temperature for 5 min. Record the absorbance of the solution at 652 nm.

The accuracy of the procedure was assessed using the sample-adding standard's recovery rate to confirm its practicality. For determining H2O2, milk and tap water were the chosen samples. After diluting the milk multiple times, H2O2 solutions were added to the samples at concentrations of 1500 nM, 1750 nM, and 2000 nM, respectively, to prevent casein from interfering. The labeled samples were then subjected to UV–VIS spectroscopic analysis.

The formation of N-GQDs/Fe3O4/Co3O4 nanoparticles and the proposed colorimetric sensing method for detecting H2O2 are shown in Scheme 1. Dispersed spherical N-GQDs/Fe3O4 nanoparticles were obtained by in-situ synthesis. Secondly, Co3O4 was deposited on the surface of N-GQDs/Fe3O4 by precipitation method. The obtained core-shell nanoparticles can oxidize TMB in the presence of H2O2 to turn it blue.

Fig. 1A and B show TEM images of individual Fe3O4 and N-GQDs/Fe3O4 nanoparticles. It can be seen from the figure that the average particle size of individual Fe3O4 nanoparticles is about 150 nm and agglomerates together, while after doping N-GQDs, the particle size of nanoparticles is reduced to 81 nm, and the dispersion is good. This is due to the rich hydroxyl group on the surface of N-GQDs, which can act as a reducing agent and stabilizer [28–30] to control the growth of nanoparticles, control their growth rate, and prevent particle aggregation [31]. In situ, synthesis, N-GQDs were added to the precursor of Fe3O4 nanoparticles to control the growth of Fe3O4 nanoparticles during the reaction, so the size of the prepared N-GQDs/Fe3O4 nanoparticles is smaller than that of the Fe3O4 nanoparticles alone. In addition, it can also be seen from Fig. 1B that, compared with Fe3O4 nanoparticles with smooth surfaces, N-GQDs doping makes the nanoparticles become irregular and the surface is similar to that of many small nanoparticles, which also proves that N-GQDs has a significant impact on the crystal growth of hybrid nanoparticles [32]. Fig. 1C is a TEM image of Co3O4 alone, from which it can be found that Co3O4 nanoparticles are relatively aggregated, while N-GQDs/Fe3O4/Co3O4 nanoparticles show a shape similar to litchi balls with good dispersion, indicating that the core-shell structure and the introduction of N-GQDs solve the problem of Fe3O4 and Co3O4 agglomeration. In addition, the element spectrum analysis of core-shell nanoparticles was further conducted, and it was found that C, N, and O elements were not only distributed on the nanoparticles but also existed on the edges of the nanoparticles. Thus, it can be concluded that N-GQDS was not only involved in the synthesis of nanoparticles but also successfully coated on the periphery of the nanoparticles, further proving the successful preparation of core-shell structures. In addition, the presence of C, N, O, Fe, and Co in core-shell nanoparticles was further demonstrated by energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) (Fig. S1).

The composition of the core-shell nanoparticles was verified by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), as shown in Fig. 2 A.C1s (284.8 eV), O1s (530.55 eV), N1s (399.53 eV), Co2p (780.71 eV and 796.47 eV), Fe2p (711.58 eV and 724.16 eV). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. 2B), which was deconvoluted into three peaks, the peaks located at 284.25 eV, 285.15 eV, and 288.67 eV corresponded to the C-C bond, the C-O bond, and the O-CO bond, respectively, suggesting the presence of carbon quantum dots with functional groups (CQD) [33]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of N1s (Fig. 2E) peaks were located at 399.52 eV, 400.52 eV, and 402.17 e, respectively, in the form of pyridine N, pyrrole N, and graphite N groups. The peak at 529.94 eV of the high-resolution XPS spectrum of O1s (Fig. 2 F) peaks were attributed to carboxylate bonding (-COOH), and the peak at 531.58 The peak at 531.58 is attributed to metal-oxygen atom clusters (M-O), indicating that Co3O4 was successfully deposited on the surface of N-GQDs/Fe3O4. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of Co2p (Fig. 2D) has peaks centered at 780.42 eV corresponding to Co2p3/2, and peaks centered at 796.28 eV corresponding to Co2p1/2, with two satellite peaks at 786.19 eV and 802.72 eV. the energy difference between the Co2p3/2 and Co2p1/2 splits is 15.5 eV, indicating the presence of both Co2+/Co3+ and confirming that both Co3O4 and CoO are present with the material, with Co3O4 being the major cobalt oxide phase [34]. The relative content of Co2+ was calculated to be 21 % and that of Co3+ to be 17 %, and the increase in Co2+ accelerated the transfer of electrons [35]. The synergistic effect produced by the mixed modification of Co2+/Co3+ increases the stability of the material [36]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 2 C) of Fe2p shows the peak of Fe2p3/2 at 711.58 eV. Fe2p1/2 peak at 724.16 eV, and satellite peaks at 716.83 eV. where the binding energies at 710.69 eV and 723.39 eV are attributed to Fe(II), and 712.99 eV and 725.28 eV are attributed to Fe(III) [37].

To compare the catalytic capacity of peroxidase of N-GQDs/Fe3O4/Co3O4, Co3O4, and N-GQDs/Fe3O4, their specific surface area was determined by N2 adsorption/desorption, as shown in Fig. 3. The calculated specific surface area of N-GQDs/Fe3O4/Co3O4 was 69.31 m2/g. The specific surface area of Co3O4 was 56.77 m2/g, and that of N-GQDs/Fe3O4 was 44.87 m2/g, indicating that the unique core-shell structure significantly increased the specific surface area of nanoparticles and exposed more active sites, thus improving the catalytic activity of enzymes.

To further demonstrate the composition of N-GQDs/Fe3O4/Co3O4 nanoparticles, the XRD (X-ray diffraction) patterns were analyzed as shown in Fig. S3, where stronger diffraction peaks appeared at 30 °, 35 °, 43 °, 53 °, 57 °, and 62 °, which corresponded to (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (511), respectively, of Fe3O4, (440). The diffraction peaks appearing at 30 °, 36 °, 45 °, 57 °, and 66 ° correspond to (220), (311), (400), (422), and (440) of Co3O4. In addition, diffraction peaks of CoO appeared in the prepared Co3O4 at 42.24 ° and 61.28 ° corresponding to (200) and (220) of CoO, respectively, indicating the presence of some CoO in the prepared Co3O4, which is consistent with the correspondence with the XPS results. It has been shown that Co3O4 nanoparticles start to transform into CoO at a temperature of 350 °C [38], while the calcination temperature in this experiment was 450 °C, so some of the Co3O4 nanoparticles were reduced to CoO during the calcination process. The diffraction peaks appearing at 35°, 43°, and 62° in the XRD spectrum of the N-GQDs/Fe3O4@Co3O4 nanoparticles were from (311), (400), and (440) of Fe3O4, the characteristic peaks appearing at 36° are reflected from (311) of Co3O4. The characteristic peaks appearing at 42° are reflected from some of the CoO present in Co3O4.

The hysteresis loop proves that the core-shell nanoparticles prepared by Fe3O4 alone and N-GQDs/Fe3O4 as the magnetic core are all magnetic, as shown in Figure S4. Among them, N-GQDs/Fe3O4 as a magnetic core has the highest saturation magnetization, which may be due to the reduction of the size of the prepared N-GQDs/Fe3O4 nanoparticles after the addition of N-GQDs, thus exhibiting magnetic behavior different from that of Fe3O4. In addition, since the surface of N-GQDs contains many oxygen-containing groups, the magnetic behavior of N-GQDs is different from that of Fe3O4. It changes the magnetic moment of the interaction, resulting in a magnetic enhancement [39]. Although the magnetic properties of the prepared core-shell nanoparticles are minimal, the calculated saturation magnetization of the core-shell nanoparticles is 16.1 emu/g, which is magnetic enough to be magnetically separated by common magnets. Because the nanoparticles contain materials with ferromagnetic and antiferromagnetic components, the coercivity is not equal, but their high saturation magnetization enables them to respond quickly to external magnetic fields, which is conducive to their effective separation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase mimetic activity of core-shell nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Due to its unique core-shell structure and the synergistic effect between Co3O4 and N-GQDs/Fe3O4, the prepared N-GQDs/Fe3O4 nanoparticles have excellent peroxidase activity. To evaluate the catalytic activity of core-shell nanoparticles, UV–visible absorption spectroscopy was used to measure the catalytic ability.

N-GQDs/Fe3O4/Co3O4 nanoparticles can oxidize TMB in the presence of H2O2, causing it to turn blue, resulting in a distinct absorption peak at 652 nm, as can be seen in Fig. 4. In contrast, in the absence of N-GQDs/Fe3O4/Co3O4 nanoparticles, TMB cannot be oxidized by H2O2. Similarly, in the absence of TMB, no color change or absorption peak at 652 nm was detected. In addition, because the prepared core-shell nanoparticles have weak oxidase activity, TMB can also be oxidized in the absence of H2O2, but only weak blue production, with a small change at 652 nm. Due to their peroxidase activity, Co3O4 and N-GQDs/Fe3O4 alone can produce a color change in the presence of TMB and H2O2 and have a significant absorption peak at 652 nm. The peroxidase activity of N-GQDs/Fe3O4 is not as strong as that of Co3O4. However, the enzyme activity of these two core-shell nanoparticles is weaker than that of the prepared core-shell nanoparticles, which is due to the larger specific surface area of the core-shell nanoparticles, the exposure of more active sites, and the synergistic effect between N-GQDs/Fe3O4 and Co3O4, which enhances the enzyme activity. In addition, it can also be seen from the TEM image that individual Co3O4 is clustered together, while N-GQDs/Fe3O4/Co3O4 is uniformly dispersed in the shell due to the presence of nuclei, thus increasing the enzyme activity.

As with natural peroxidases, temperature, pH, TMB concentration, and nanoenzyme concentration all impact the activity of manufactured nanoenzymes. Thus, the effects of pH, TMB concentration, temperature, and nanoenzyme concentration on the core-shell nanoparticles' peroxidase activity were studied. Fig. S5A illustrates how pH affects the peroxidase activity of the nanoparticles， catalytic activity rises with pH and then falls, culminating at pH=6. The impact of TMB concentration on catalytic activity was also studied; as Fig. S5B illustrates, catalytic activity increased as TMB concentration rose, and it tended to stabilize at concentrations of 0.25 mM or higher.

The effect of temperature on the peroxidase activity of nanoparticles was investigated, as shown in Fig.S5C, the catalytic activity increases and then decreases with increasing temperature in the temperature range of 15–55 °C, and reaches the maximum value at a temperature of 25 °C. This is because the increase in temperature will provide more energy for the nanoparticles to bind to the substrate, thus increasing the catalytic reaction rate, but too high a temperature will decompose H2O2, which is detrimental to the reaction [40].

The concentration of nanoparticles was also optimized, as shown in Fig.S5D, the catalytic activity increased and gradually leveled off as the concentration of nanoparticles increased. Since the catalytic activity reaches saturation as the quantity of nanoenzymes increases, an excessive concentration of nanoenzymes will allow UV light to diffuse and will disrupt the impact of color development [41]. Furthermore, the impact of time on the catalytic activity was also examined; as seen in Fig. S5E, the catalytic activity progressively rose over time until leveling out. Consequently, the appeal study's findings demonstrated that the conditions of pH=6, TMB concentration of 0.25 mM, temperature of 25 °C, nanoparticle concentration of 25 μg/mL, and duration of 5 min produced the maximum catalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic mechanism and steady-state kinetic studies
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the catalytic mechanism of peroxidase activity of core-shell nanoparticles, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) analysis of the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), the main reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the reaction system of the core-shell nanoparticles, was carried out by using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrrolidinyl-N-oxide (DMPO) spin trapping agent as shown in Fig. 5A, a characteristic 1:2:2:1 signal of ·OH was generated in the presence of DMPO, indicating the generation of ·OH in the reaction system. In addition, a 1:1:1:1 characteristic signal of ·O2 - appeared in methanol solution as shown in Fig. 5B, but the intensity of the signal was significantly weakened compared to that of ·OH, indicating that a small amount of ·O2 - was generated in the reaction system. Subsequent verification of 1O2 by 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine oxide (TEMP) detected a 1:1:1 characteristic signal (Fig. 5C), demonstrating that a small amount of 1O2 was also present in the system [42]. This fact suggests that N-GQDs/Fe3O4/Co3O4 nanoparticles can catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 into ·OH, ·O2 -, and 1O2 radicals, but the nature of the peroxidase-like activity of the nanoparticles mainly originates from the generation of ·OH radicals.

In addition, the mechanism by which core-shell nanoparticles enhance peroxidase activity is discussed. Fig. 4D shows a schematic diagram of the catalytic process of N-GQDs/Fe3O4/Co3O4 nanoparticles. The catalytic mechanism of Co3O4 was analyzed and discussed since the peroxidase activity of different systems was obtained by examining the peroxidase activity in the previous experiments and the enhancement of the enzyme-like activity of the N-GQDs/Fe3O4/Co3O4 nanoparticles was mainly related to Co3O4. In the reaction system, Co(II) on Co3O4 nanomaterials transfers electrons to a molecule of H2O2 into Co(III), which reduces H2O2 to ·OH [43]. In addition, H2O2 as an electron acceptor reacts with Co3O4 nanomaterials to form OH- [44]. In addition to the appeal process, H2O2 will react with ·OH to generate ·O2 -, due to the oxidation reaction between ·O2 - and ·OH, which will lead to the generation of 1O2 in the system, which is the reason why there will be a small amount of ·O2 - and 1O2 generated in the system, which is in agreement with the results obtained in the EPR analysis. The specific formula is as follows:
(1) Co2++H2O2→Co3++OH-+·OH
(2) ·O2 -+·OH→1O2+OH-

By changing the concentration of TMB or H2O2 in the reaction system, steady-state kinetic studies were carried out to examine the kinetic mechanism of the peroxidase-like activity of N-GQDs/Fe3O4/Co3O4. The Michaelis-Menten curves for two different substrates in the reaction system are shown in Figs. 6A and 6C, respectively, and the corresponding Lineweaver-Burk curves are shown in Figs. 6B and 6D. By fitting the values of the reaction velocity and substrate concentration to the Michaelis-Menten equation, the Michaelis-Menten constant (K
m ) and the maximum initial velocity (v
max
). The magnitude of K
m is related to the affinity between the substrate and the catalyst, and a lower value of K
m represents a stronger affinity of the catalyst for the substrate. The K
m values of the prepared core-shell nanoparticles for TMB and H2O2 were 0.39 mM and 1.17 mM, respectively, which were much lower than that of HRP, indicating that the prepared core-shell nanoparticles had a stronger affinity for the substrate than HRP ( Table 1). This is because there is only one active site in the HRP molecule [45], whereas there are many catalytically active sites on the surface of the core-shell nanoparticles. By comparing with other previously reported nanomaterials, the nanoparticles prepared in this study showed a higher affinity for H2O2, which indicates that the introduction of N-GQDs and the preparation of the core-shell structure effectively improved their affinity for H2O2. In addition, Kcat (Kcat=V
max
/[S]) was calculated. As can be seen from Table 1, the prepared Co3O4 nanoparticles had a larger Kcat than HRP and Co3O4, indicating a higher peroxidase activity.

The peroxidase activity of the nanoparticles was used to measure the H2O2 concentration. As seen in Fig. 7A, the absorbance of the reaction system was ascertained under ideal experimental circumstances. As the quantity of H2O2 grew, so did the absorbance in the 20–2500 nM range. In the 20–2500 nM detection range, the absorbance exhibited a strong linear correlation with the H2O2 concentration (Fig. 7B), and the detection limit was determined to be 2.3 nM. Compared with other sensors ( Table 2), the prepared core-shell nanoparticles had better sensing performance and lower detection limits. To investigate the specificity of nanoparticles for the detection of H2O2, specificity experiments were carried out, as shown in Fig. 7C, even though the concentration of other interfering substances (Glycine, L-histidine, L-arginine, Na+, K+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Mg2+, and Zn2+, KMnO4, K2CrO7) was 10 times of the concentration of H2O2 at the time of the concentration of the H2O2 concentration, there was still a significant change of the absorbance, so the nanoparticles had a high selectivity for the detection of H2O2 has high selectivity.

Long-term stability is an additional metric for assessing the nanoenzymes. After keeping the nanoparticles at low temperatures for 26 days, the relative activity decreased by 30 %, as shown in Fig. 7D, which proves that the N-GQDs/Fe3O4/Co3O4 nanoparticles have long-term stability. Since the prepared nanoparticles were magnetic, experiments were conducted on their cycle repeatability, and it was found that there was a significant decrease in the relative activity after three cycles (Fig. 7E), which showed good recyclability. The appealing results show that the prepared nanosensors exhibit good recyclability and stability, providing a cost-effective and environmentally friendly detection platform for the detection of H2O2.

To verify the workability of nanoparticles in practical applications, H2O2 in milk and tap water was detected using N-GQDs/Fe3O4/Co3O4.Since the main constituent of milk is casein, which can interfere with the experiment, the samples were treated by dilution and centrifugation to remove other organic matter such as fats, proteins, and so on, before the test. And it was added to the TMB-H2O2 system. The findings are displayed in Table 3, where the recoveries of H2O2 in tap water were 97.51 %-103.74 % and in milk were 98.4–104 %. Both recoveries had a modest relative standard deviation, indicating that the produced nanoparticle sensors may be used for real H2O2 detection [47–49].

In summary, magnetic N-GQDs/Fe3O4/Co3O4 core-shell nanoparticles were prepared by in-situ synthesis and precipitation in this study. The nanoparticles have excellent peroxidase activity. Because N-GQDs is doped to disperse Co3O4 in the shell, the unique core-shell structure exposes more active sites, making the prepared material not only able to detect H2O2 in a short time, but also lower the detection limit compared with the previous nanomaterials. Therefore, the prepared nanoparticles showed high catalytic activity, anti-interference and stability in the detection of H2O2, and achieved satisfactory results in the detection of actual samples, providing an efficient and rapid detection platform for the on-site detection of H2O2, and expanding its application prospects in environmental and food analysis.

Nali Chen: Writing – review & editing. Ran Meng: Writing – review & editing. Shaohui Li: Writing – review & editing. Xuefang Lei: Writing – review & editing. Dongxia Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Xibin Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision. Peiqing Guo: Writing – review & editing. Mingzhu Qiu: Writing – review & editing. Yujin Li: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (201165016, and 21265018); the Science and Technology Support Projects of Gansu Province (Nos. 1011GKCA025, and 1208RJZM289); and the Postgraduate Research Funding Projects of Northwest Normal University (2021KYZZ02140), and Key Laboratory of Resource Environment and Sustainable Development of Oasis, Gansu Province (GORS202301).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2024.134061.
